High winds combined with fresh snow from previous days caused slick roads and near whiteout conditions as localized ground blizzards developed.  One person was injured in Summit County when she drove off Interstate 70 in whiteout conditions.  Strong winds blew snow across the runways at Centennial Airport which glazed over and formed areas of black ice.  Two planes were damaged when they slid off the runway while landing. A weather disturbance moved northeast across northern New Mexico, bringing snow to the mountains, high valleys, and plains of south central and southeast Colorado starting the morning of the 2nd through the afternoon of the 3rd.  Snow accumulations ranged from 4 to 6 inches across parts of the southeast plains, the Palmer Divide, and southern portions of the San Luis Valley.  Snowfall reached 9 inches at Rye in the Wet Mountains, 17 inches at Cuchara in the Southern Sangre De Cristo Mountains, and 22 inches at Wolf Creek Ski Area in the Eastern San Juan Mountains.  The rest of the southeast plains received 1 to 3 inches amounts, including much of the Colorado Springs and Pueblo areas.

Another round of high winds occurred over portions of north central and northeast Colorado.  Electrical outages were reported in Breckenridge as some power lines were downed.  High wind reports included:  84 mph (73 kts) at Carter Lake, 71 mph (62 kts) at Blue Mountain, Red Feather Lakes and 4 miles south of Livermore, 70 mph (61 kts) at Breckenridge and Broomfield, with 59 mph (51 kts) reported 4 miles east of Haxtun.

A deep east to northeast flow associated with a vigorous upper level low pressure system over the Four Corners area, combined with a strong Arctic airmass in the central plains, to create deadly blizzard conditions across all of eastern Colorado.  Snowfall totals along the Urban Corridor and adjacent plains generally ranged from 14 to 30 inches.  The heaviest snowfall occurred in the foothills west and southwest of Denver where 2 to 4 feet of snow was reported.  In addition, sustained winds to 40 mph (35 kts) with gusts as high as 60 mph (52 kts) caused zero visibilities and deadly wind chill temperatures to dip between 25 below and 40 below zero.  The blizzard stirred up snowdrifts ranging from 4 to 10 feet in depth.  Several major highways and interstates were closed as conditions became life threatening and travel impossible.  Red Cross shelters were set up for hundreds of travellers who became stranded and were forced to abandon their vehicles.  In the mountains, sustained winds to 70 mph (61 kts) with peak wind gusts to 110 mph (96 kts) were recorded above 12,000 feet at the Breckenridge Ski Area.  The blizzard was responsible, either directly or indirectly, for the deaths of 4 people in northeast Colorado.  One man died of carbon monoxide poisoning when he became stranded in his vehicle and the snow clogged the exhaust pipe.  A woman died when she lost control of the car in the blinding storm.  In addition, 2 elderly men died from heart attacks while shovelling snow.At Denver International Airport (DIA), 4,000 travellers were stranded when the airport was forced to shut down.  At least 120 cars were abandoned along Pena Blvd, the only arterial leading into and out of DIA.  The blizzard cost air carriers at least $20 million.  In Weld County, nearly 3,000 head of cattle died in the storm resulting in an estimated $1.5 million loss.Some of the more impressive snowfall totals included:  51 inches at Coal Creek Canyon, 48 inches at Silver Spruce Ranch and Monument Hill, 42 inches at Intercanyon, 37 inches at Sedalia, 36 inches at Foxton, 35 inches at Aspen Springs and Conifer, 32 inches at Elizabeth, 31 inches at Eldorado Springs, southeast Aurora and Englewood, with 30 inches reported at Allenspark, Sunshine Canyon and Table Mesa near Boulder.

A strong winter storm system moved into Colorado from the west, bringing an abundance of moisture and gusty winds from 20 to 40 mph. The storm brought accumulations of 12 to 20 inches to the Eastern San Juan and La Garita mountains during the morning and afternoon of the 27th. The San Luis valley received 6 to 18 inches of snow the afternoon and evening of the 27th. The system then moved east and brought heavy snow to the Sangre de Cristo mountains and adjacent foothills and plains the evening of the 27th into the night of the 28th. A band of intense snowfall lasted for many hours over the Wet mountains, Wet Mountain Valley, southern Sangre de Cristo mountains, and Walsenburg area.  Total accumulations in those areas ranged from around 30 inches in the Wet Mountain Valley, around three feet in the southern Sangre de Cristo mountains and La Veta Pass, and at Walsenburg, Rye, and Beulah, to 45 inches at the 9,000 foot level in the Wet Mountains.  Nearly 30 inches of snow fell near Divide in Teller county.  Eight to 10 inches fell along the Palmer Divide, with generally less than 5 inches in the Colorado Springs area.  Pueblo tallied about 5 inches. The severe winter conditions closed numerous roads and highways, including Interstate 25 from Pueblo south to the New Mexico state line. Four thousand homes were without power in Pueblo West and Turkey Creek in Pueblo county when strong winds and heavy wet snow brought down hundreds of power poles. Ninety-two year old Marie Brandenburg of Westcliffe in Custer county died when she accidently locked herself out of her house, then slipped and fell by her back porch.  She froze to death in the high winds and heavy snow.
1.50 inches of rain fell within a half hour, along with half-inch diameter hail and strong winds.  This resulted in flooding of pastureland and crop damage.

CZ_NAME: UPPER PURGATOIRE RIVER BASIN/TRINIDAD AND VICINITY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific weather system moved across the Rockies and into the central plains of the U.S.from the 8th through the 10th. Abundant moisture was wrapped around the system, and brought snow to the southwestern and south central mountains the afternoon of the 8th through the 10th...and to the southeastern plains the morning of the 9th through the 10th.  Totals from a persistent band of snow ranged from 4 inches at the Pueblo Memorial Airport to 7 inches at Manzanola and Swink in Otero county. Fort Reynolds in Pueblo county reported 9 1/2 inches of accumulation by the afternoon of the 9th.  Across Monument Ridge, north of Colorado Springs, storm totals ranged from 10 to 18 inches in the Monument and Black Forest region east into northeast El Paso county.  Near blizzard conditions were experienced during the afternoon of the 10th in eastern El Paso county, forcing U.S. Highway 24 to close for a time.  Other snowfall totals included around one foot at Beulah and 20 to 25 inches around Rye in Pueblo county in the Wet Mountains.  Twenty two inches fell at Aspen Acres Campground in Custer county, and a remarkable 44 inches at Cuchara at 9,000 feet in the Southern Sangre De Cristo Mountains.
Outflow winds from an approaching thunderstorm sent five airline baggage carts travelling about 50 feet before plowing into a glider plane.

Wind driven hail up to three-quarters of an inch in diameter decimated fields of ripening wheat as well as gardens.  The siding on the west exposure of some buildings in the area were dented and had paint chipped off.

CZ_NAME: ELKHEAD AND PARK MOUNTAINS/FLAT TOPS/WHITE RIVER PLATEAU / E RIO BLANCO / NE GARFIELD / X NW EAGLE / ROUTT EXCEPT C XS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving Pacific trough produced 4 to 7 inches of snow in most mountain areas of western Colorado as well as a few valleys above the 6000 foot level.  The heaviest reported accumulation was in the northern Uncompahgre Plateau with about a foot of new snow.

CZ_NAME: GRAND AND BATTLEMENT MESAS / X SC GARFIELD / E MESA EXCEPT X NE / N DELTA

CZ_NAME: EASTERN LARIMER AND NORTHWESTERN WELD COUNTIES / FORT COLLINS AND VICINITY / E LARIMER / NW WELD
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of high winds occurred over portions of north central and northeast Colorado.  Electrical outages were reported in Breckenridge as some power lines were downed.  High wind reports included:  84 mph (73 kts) at Carter Lake, 71 mph (62 kts) at Blue Mountain, Red Feather Lakes and 4 miles south of Livermore, 70 mph (61 kts) at Breckenridge and Broomfield, with 59 mph (51 kts) reported 4 miles east of Haxtun.

CZ_NAME: NORTHERN FRONT RANGE FOOTHILLS/UPPER LARAMIE AND CACHE LA POUDRE RIVER BASINS / C BOULDER / NE GILPIN / C LARIMER
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of high winds occurred over portions of north central and northeast Colorado.  Electrical outages were reported in Breckenridge as some power lines were downed.  High wind reports included:  84 mph (73 kts) at Carter Lake, 71 mph (62 kts) at Blue Mountain, Red Feather Lakes and 4 miles south of Livermore, 70 mph (61 kts) at Breckenridge and Broomfield, with 59 mph (51 kts) reported 4 miles east of Haxtun.
A section of County Road 222 was washed out.
Heavy rains resulted in a rapid rise of the Florida River, washing out a temporary bridge along county road 228.
Lightning struck the roof of a two story house, causing the house to catch on fire.
A flash flood produced a mud slide which completely buried a 30 foot stretch of high 82 near Basalt with mud 2 to 4 feet deep.  It took state road crews nearly seven hours to clear the highway.
Heavy rains resulted in a mud slide which blocked a road in a subdivision.

A woman was struck by lightning on a mountain ridge while riding a mountain bike with three friends.  The friends were uninjured.  The victim was in between two other cyclists, separated about 15 yards apart from each other.  The fourth cyclist was 75 yards further up the ridge when the lightning struck.

A flash flood came roaring down from a creek north of Marble, crossing the main road on the northwest side of town before spilling into the Crystal River.  The flood waters crossed a 100 yard section of the road, FR314,  and was reported to be several feet deep.  Mud and debris two feet deep remained on the road after the waters receded.  A barn in the path of the flood waters was damaged, and many Aspen trees were leveled.
Lightning ignited a range fire near Towaoc.

Heavy rains resulted in a flash flood with a large amount of mud and other debris traversing highway 131.  Mud up to 2 feet deep remained along a 60 foot section of the highway after the flood waters subsided.
Heavy rains caused a flash flood which washed out a road near Gladstone.
Heavy rains resulted in water and debris about a half foot deep running across the highway near Rio Blanco, temporarily stopping traffic.

Heavy rains brought high water mixed with mud and debris across several residences, destroying a gazebo, greenhouse, and shed.  A driveway was washed out and a tennis court was severely damaged. A weather disturbance moved northeast across northern New Mexico, bringing snow to the mountains, high valleys, and plains of south central and southeast Colorado starting the morning of the 2nd through the afternoon of the 3rd.  Snow accumulations ranged from 4 to 6 inches across parts of the southeast plains, the Palmer Divide, and southern portions of the San Luis Valley.  Snowfall reached 9 inches at Rye in the Wet Mountains, 17 inches at Cuchara in the Southern Sangre De Cristo Mountains, and 22 inches at Wolf Creek Ski Area in the Eastern San Juan Mountains.  The rest of the southeast plains received 1 to 3 inches amounts, including much of the Colorado Springs and Pueblo areas.

CZ_NAME: EASTERN DOUGLAS AND WESTERN ELBERT COUNTIES/CASTLE ROCK AND VICINITY

Heavy snow fell portions of the northern mountains and in the foothills and Monument Ridge area west and south of Denver.  Gusty northerly winds 20 to 35 mph blew across portions of  Monument Ridge causing some blowing and drifting snow.Snowfall totals included:  14.5 inches at Larkspur, 14 inches near Deckers, 13 inches at Breckenridge Ski Area, 12 inches at Castle Rock and Sedalia, 11 inches at Elizabeth, with 10 inches reported at Buffalo Creek,  3 miles southwest of Conifer, Keystone and 11 miles southwest of Morrison.  Elsewhere accumulations generally ranged from 5 to 8 inches.

A deep east to northeast flow associated with a vigorous upper level low pressure system over the Four Corners area, combined with a strong Arctic airmass in the central plains, to create deadly blizzard conditions across all of eastern Colorado.  Snowfall totals along the Urban Corridor and adjacent plains generally ranged from 14 to 30 inches.  The heaviest snowfall occurred in the foothills west and southwest of Denver where 2 to 4 feet of snow was reported.  In addition, sustained winds to 40 mph (35 kts) with gusts as high as 60 mph (52 kts) caused zero visibilities and deadly wind chill temperatures to dip between 25 below and 40 below zero.  The blizzard stirred up snowdrifts ranging from 4 to 10 feet in depth.  Several major highways and interstates were closed as conditions became life threatening and travel impossible.  Red Cross shelters were set up for hundreds of travellers who became stranded and were forced to abandon their vehicles.  In the mountains, sustained winds to 70 mph (61 kts) with peak wind gusts to 110 mph (96 kts) were recorded above 12,000 feet at the Breckenridge Ski Area.  The blizzard was responsible, either directly or indirectly, for the deaths of 4 people in northeast Colorado.  One man died of carbon monoxide poisoning when he became stranded in his vehicle and the snow clogged the exhaust pipe.  A woman died when she lost control of the car in the blinding storm.  In addition, 2 elderly men died from heart attacks while shovelling snow.At Denver International Airport (DIA), 4,000 travellers were stranded when the airport was forced to shut down. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong north to northeast upslope flow developed over northeast Colorado as a storm system tracked across northern New Mexico.  Heavy snow fell portions of the northern mountains and in the foothills and Monument Ridge area west and south of Denver.  Gusty northerly winds 20 to 35 mph blew across portions of  Monument Ridge causing some blowing and drifting snow.Snowfall totals included:  14.5 inches at Larkspur, 14 inches near Deckers, 13 inches at Breckenridge Ski Area, 12 inches at Castle Rock and Sedalia, 11 inches at Elizabeth, with 10 inches reported at Buffalo Creek,  3 miles southwest of Conifer, Keystone and 11 miles southwest of Morrison.  Elsewhere accumulations generally ranged from 5 to 8 inches.

A moist Pacific weather system moved across the Rockies and into the central plains of the U.S.from the 8th through the 10th. Abundant moisture was wrapped around the system, and brought snow to the southwestern and south central mountains the afternoon of the 8th through the 10th...and to the southeastern plains the morning of the 9th through the 10th.  Totals from a persistent band of snow ranged from 4 inches at the Pueblo Memorial Airport to 7 inches at Manzanola and Swink in Otero county. Fort Reynolds in Pueblo county reported 9 1/2 inches of accumulation by the afternoon of the 9th.  Across Monument Ridge, north of Colorado Springs, storm totals ranged from 10 to 18 inches in the Monument and Black Forest region east into northeast El Paso county.  Near blizzard conditions were experienced during the afternoon of the 10th in eastern El Paso county, forcing U.S. Highway 24 to close for a time.  Other snowfall totals included around one foot at Beulah and 20 to 25 inches around Rye in Pueblo county in the Wet Mountains.  Twenty two inches fell at Aspen Acres Campground in Custer county, and a remarkable 44 inches at Cuchara at 9,000 feet in the Southern Sangre De Cristo Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system combined with arctic air to bring widespread snowfall to south central and southeast Colorado, starting the evening of the 13th and lasting through the afternoon of the 14th. Snowfall accumulations ranged from 6 to 12 inches across the mountains and high valleys, with 4 to 8 inches across the foothills and nearby plains.  The heaviest amounts occurred in the southern Sangre de Cristo mountains where up to 12 inches fell around Cuchara and Stonewall.  A narrow band of intense snow fell near Del Norte in the San Luis Valley, bringing up to 10 inches.

An abundance of moisture across the state in combination with an upper level weather system over south central Colorado brought snow to most of south central and southeastern Colorado. The heaviest hit areas were the La Garita and Eastern San Juan mountains, the Upper Arkansas River Valley, and the northern portions of the San Luis Valley. Snow amounts of 5 to 9 inches were reported.
Heavy rains produced flash floods in the Craig area which washed out sections of some county roads and also blocked off some roads due to mud slides.

A strong winter storm system moved into Colorado from the west, bringing an abundance of moisture and gusty winds from 20 to 40 mph. The storm brought accumulations of 12 to 20 inches to the Eastern San Juan and La Garita mountains during the morning and afternoon of the 27th. The San Luis valley received 6 to 18 inches of snow the afternoon and evening of the 27th. The system then moved east and brought heavy snow to the Sangre de Cristo mountains and adjacent foothills and plains the evening of the 27th into the night of the 28th. A band of intense snowfall lasted for many hours over the Wet mountains, Wet Mountain Valley, southern Sangre de Cristo mountains, and Walsenburg area.  Total accumulations in those areas ranged from around 30 inches in the Wet Mountain Valley, around three feet in the southern Sangre de Cristo mountains and La Veta Pass, and at Walsenburg, Rye, and Beulah, to 45 inches at the 9,000 foot level in the Wet Mountains.  Nearly 30 inches of snow fell near Divide in Teller county.  Eight to 10 inches fell along the Palmer Divide, with generally less than 5 inches in the Colorado Springs area.  Pueblo tallied about 5 inches. The severe winter conditions closed numerous roads and highways, including Interstate 25 from Pueblo south to the New Mexico state line. Four thousand homes were without power in Pueblo West and Turkey Creek in Pueblo county when strong winds and heavy wet snow brought down hundreds of power poles. Ninety-two year old Marie Brandenburg of Westcliffe in Custer county died when she accidently locked herself out of her house, then slipped and fell by her back porch.  She froze to death in the high winds and heavy snow.

A weather disturbance moved northeast across northern New Mexico, bringing snow to the mountains, high valleys, and plains of south central and southeast Colorado starting the morning of the 2nd through the afternoon of the 3rd.  Snow accumulations ranged from 4 to 6 inches across parts of the southeast plains, the Palmer Divide, and southern portions of the San Luis Valley.  Snowfall reached 9 inches at Rye in the Wet Mountains, 17 inches at Cuchara in the Southern Sangre De Cristo Mountains, and 22 inches at Wolf Creek Ski Area in the Eastern San Juan Mountains.  The rest of the southeast plains received 1 to 3 inches amounts, including much of the Colorado Springs and Pueblo areas.

Heavy rain and debris in the Arkansas River caused above flood stage readings in western Prowers county in and near Lamar for a few hours.  The flood waters caused no known damage, but did come close to a few houses on the north side of Lamar.

CZ_NAME: CENTRAL YAMPA RIVER BASIN/CRAIG-HAYDEN AND VICINITY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific trough resulted in 4 to 10 inches of snow in the western Colorado mountains above the 7000 foot level and 3 to 8 inches of snow in many valleys between 5000 and 7000 feet above sea level.  Some heavier totals included 24 inches at Buffalo Pass northeast of Steamboat Springs, 17 inches above Aspen, 14 inches at Chattanooga on the south side of Red Mountain Pass, 13 inches on the Grand Mesa, 13 inches at Climax, and 12 inches at Crested Butte.

An upper level storm system combined with arctic air to bring widespread snowfall to south central and southeast Colorado, starting the evening of the 13th and lasting through the afternoon of the 14th. Snowfall accumulations ranged from 6 to 12 inches across the mountains and high valleys, with 4 to 8 inches across the foothills and nearby plains.  The heaviest amounts occurred in the southern Sangre de Cristo mountains where up to 12 inches fell around Cuchara and Stonewall.  A narrow band of intense snow fell near Del Norte in the San Luis Valley, bringing up to 10 inches.

A moist Pacific weather system moved across the Rockies and into the central plains of the U.S.from the 8th through the 10th. Abundant moisture was wrapped around the system, and brought snow to the southwestern and south central mountains the afternoon of the 8th through the 10th...and to the southeastern plains the morning of the 9th through the 10th.  Totals from a persistent band of snow ranged from 4 inches at the Pueblo Memorial Airport to 7 inches at Manzanola and Swink in Otero county. Fort Reynolds in Pueblo county reported 9 1/2 inches of accumulation by the afternoon of the 9th.  Across Monument Ridge, north of Colorado Springs, storm totals ranged from 10 to 18 inches in the Monument and Black Forest region east into northeast El Paso county.  Near blizzard conditions were experienced during the afternoon of the 10th in eastern El Paso county, forcing U.S. Highway 24 to close for a time.  Other snowfall totals included around one foot at Beulah and 20 to 25 inches around Rye in Pueblo county in the Wet Mountains.  Twenty two inches fell at Aspen Acres Campground in Custer county, and a remarkable 44 inches at Cuchara at 9,000 feet in the Southern Sangre De Cristo Mountains.

Heavy rains resulted in rock and mud slides covering railroad tracks of the Durango and Silverton Narrow Gauge Railroad through the Animas Canyon.  The railroad was shut down for three days to allow crews to clear debris and make repairs to the railroad tracks.

Over an inch of rain with small hail fell in Durango within a 40 minute period.  1 to 2 feet of water swept through a mobile home park along Junction Creek, and 2 to 3 feet of water backed up and flooded a motel laundry.  A mud slide covered a section of highway 550 on the north side of town, closing one lane for two days before road crews were able to completely clear the road.

Flash flooding brought down an enormous amount of mud and large rocks onto a stretch of the westbound lanes of I-70 from the Palisade exit to one mile east of the exit.  Mud 3 to 4 feet deep covered the road, along with boulders the size of cars.  The westbound lanes of I-70 were closed for two days while road crews worked to clear the road and repair the damage.

CZ_NAME: NORTHERN FRONT RANGE FOOTHILLS/UPPER LARAMIE AND CACHE LA POUDRE RIVER BASINS / C BOULDER / NE GILPIN / C LARIMER
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hurricane force winds occurred on a very localized scale as an intense mountain wave formed along the foothills.  A peak wind gust of 125 mph (110 kts) was recorded at Christ Mountain, 7 miles west of Fort Collins.  In addition, a peak wind gust of 99 mph (86 kts) was reported 1 mile east of Buckhorn Mountain.  No damage was reported.

CZ_NAME: SOUTHERN EL PASO COUNTY/COLORADO SPRINGS AND VICINITY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm system moved into Colorado from the west, bringing an abundance of moisture and gusty winds from 20 to 40 mph. The storm brought accumulations of 12 to 20 inches to the Eastern San Juan and La Garita mountains during the morning and afternoon of the 27th. The San Luis valley received 6 to 18 inches of snow the afternoon and evening of the 27th. The system then moved east and brought heavy snow to the Sangre de Cristo mountains and adjacent foothills and plains the evening of the 27th into the night of the 28th. A band of intense snowfall lasted for many hours over the Wet mountains, Wet Mountain Valley, southern Sangre de Cristo mountains, and Walsenburg area.  Total accumulations in those areas ranged from around 30 inches in the Wet Mountain Valley, around three feet in the southern Sangre de Cristo mountains and La Veta Pass, and at Walsenburg, Rye, and Beulah, to 45 inches at the 9,000 foot level in the Wet Mountains.  Nearly 30 inches of snow fell near Divide in Teller county.  Eight to 10 inches fell along the Palmer Divide, with generally less than 5 inches in the Colorado Springs area.  Pueblo tallied about 5 inches. The severe winter conditions closed numerous roads and highways, including Interstate 25 from Pueblo south to the New Mexico state line. Four thousand homes were without power in Pueblo West and Turkey Creek in Pueblo county when strong winds and heavy wet snow brought down hundreds of power poles. Ninety-two year old Marie Brandenburg of Westcliffe in Custer county died when she accidently locked herself out of her house, then slipped and fell by her back porch.  She froze to death in the high winds and heavy snow.

Damaging wind ahead of an approaching storm system developed in the Front Range Foothills in the  morning, spreading out across portions of the Urban Corridor and Northeast Plains during the afternoon and early evening.  Several trees and street signs were blown down with scattered power outages reported throughout the Denver Metropolitan area.  In Arvada,  northwest of Denver, a car window was blown out by a strong wind gust. Peak wind gust reports included:  88 mph (76 kts) 8 miles west of Conifer, 71 mph (62 kts) at the NCAR facility, in the foothills southwest of Boulder, 70 mph (61 kts) at Kiowa, 59 mph (51 kts) at Limon and 58 mph (50 kts) 9 miles north of Limon and 4 miles east of Haxtun. In addition to the strong winds ahead of the storm system,  the atmosphere was very unstable.  Three severe  thunderstorms produced large hail across portions of northeast Colorado.  No damage was reported.
Heavy rains brought a torrent of water and mud across highway 139, about 3 miles north of the Garfield County line, which blocked the road for a period of time.
Heavy rains resulted in flash floods with water up to 3 feet deep over county roads just north of Rangely.
A flash flood in the oil field resulted in the break of an oil flow line.
Several county roads were badly damaged by flash floods.  A mud slide covered a section of highway 139.

Hundreds of 1.5 to 2 foot diameter pine trees were blown down.  Some of the trees fell across US highway 40 and blocked the road until state crews came with chain saws and cleared the trees from the road.
Lightning struck a tree and power pole, starting the tree on fire and destroying a power transformer.  Some electrical damage was also incurred at a nearby house.
Heavy rains resulted in a rock slide which temporarily blocked County Road 8.

CZ_NAME: WESTERN FREMONT AND NORTHERN CUSTER COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weather disturbance moved northeast across northern New Mexico, bringing snow to the mountains, high valleys, and plains of south central and southeast Colorado starting the morning of the 2nd through the afternoon of the 3rd.  Snow accumulations ranged from 4 to 6 inches across parts of the southeast plains, the Palmer Divide, and southern portions of the San Luis Valley.  Snowfall reached 9 inches at Rye in the Wet Mountains, 17 inches at Cuchara in the Southern Sangre De Cristo Mountains, and 22 inches at Wolf Creek Ski Area in the Eastern San Juan Mountains.  The rest of the southeast plains received 1 to 3 inches amounts, including much of the Colorado Springs and Pueblo areas.

CZ_NAME: GRAND VALLEY/GRAND JUNCTION AND VICINITY / NW & C MESA / X SW GARFIELD

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tornado, up to 1/4 mile wide, tracked through the high country of central Mineral county.  The 3-mile long track extended from the northeast side of Fisher mountain to Copper mountain, between 10,500 and 11,500 feet above MSL.  It snapped off or uprooted tens of thousands of spruce and pine trees and destroyed a horse trailer.  Nearby eyewitnesses described a definite twisting motion to the destructive wind.

CZ_NAME: WEST ELK AND SAWATCH MOUNTAINS/TAYLOR PARK
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was the first widespread significant Autumn snowfall in western Colorado.  Generally, 4 to 8 inches fell in the mountains above the 7000 foot level and  3 to 5  inches in some valleys above the 6000 foot level.  Heavier amounts included 10 inches at Climax, 11 inches at Mesa Lakes on the Grand Mesa, and 15 inches at Chattanooga on the south side of Red Mountain Pass.

While blizzards are not uncommon in Colorado, the widespread areal extent of this blizzard was, perhaps a one in 50 year event.  A powerful low pressure system moved in from the northwest through Utah, and settled into northern New Mexico.  Meantime, arctic air invaded eastern Colorado, setting the stage for high winds, below zero wind chill values, heavy snow, and widespread life threatening conditions.  The heavy snow began in the southwest mountains early on Friday morning the 24th.  Snow spread across the Eastern San Juan and La Garita Mountains into the San Luis Valley, Upper Arkansas Valley, Wet Mountain Valley, and Sangre de Cristo Mountains through Friday morning.  Snow began spreading across the eastern plains by midday Friday.  By Friday evening the arctic high pressure and low pressure to the south intensified, and blizzard conditions began in the eastern mountains and across the plains.  Blizzard conditions persisted, except for a brief break early Saturday morning, through Saturday night into the extreme eastern plains.  The scope and length of blizzard conditions proved fatal for several eastern Coloradans.  Three people in El Paso county perished from carbon monoxide poisoning after waiting for help to come in their snowbound vehicles for over 24 hours.  Another person froze to death in a vehicle on post at Fort Carson in the Colorado Springs area.  An elderly women in Otero county tried to walk home after her vehicle became stuck in open country.  She froze to death.  A man in Bent county froze to death in open country while hunting, or looking for other hunters.   Another man died in a vehicle accident in Pueblo during the blizzard Friday night.  Two people were injured in Colorado Springs when a canopy at a gas station collapsed under the weight of deep snow on top.  Another canopy at a gas station in Lamar collapsed, but noone was injured.  A vehicle was destroyed, though.  Thousands of people were stranded in eastern Colorado, and hundreds had to be rescued from their snowbound vehicles.  By Saturday, the Governor declared a State of Emergency.  Emergency traffic only was allowed on eastern Colorado roadways.  Rescues were made by the U.S. Army in Humvees and by helicopter, the National Guard, law enforcement, other public resources, and private citizens.  The combination of high wind and heavy snow caused power lines to come down.  Power outages occurred (and lasted up to two days) in many parts of the area, most notably in parts of Colorado Springs, in and around Pueblo, and in southeast Colorado from McClave in Bent county to Holly in Prowers county.  Hundreds of businesses and stores were closed throughout much of the weekend, and temporary sales and production loses were in the millions of dollars.  Many school districts were closed for a part, or all of the following week, as the digging out process continued.  Extra costs for snow removel in both the private and public sector from overtime wages certainly ran into the millions of dollars areawide.  Not only did humans suffer, but there was a widespread die off of range cattle.  Estimates of around 20,000 dead cattle and calves were given.  Untold numbers were saved by hay drops carried out by the Army and National Guard.  Removal of dead cattle and feeding of remaining cattle cost millions of dollars.  Snow amounts with the blizzard were impressive.  One to two feet fell in the Eastern San Juan, La Garita, and Sangre de Cristo Mountains.  However, the San Luis Valley received only a few inches of snow.  The Upper Arkansas Valley had six to 12 inches of snow.  Generally between one and two feet fell from the Rampart Range and southern Colorado Springs, south through Pueblo, down to Trinidad, and throughout the southeast Plains.  However, parts of the Wet Mountains, Beulah, Walsenburg, and some areas around La Junta had around three feet of snow.  From northern Colorado Springs to the Palmer Divide, amounts were excessive.  In northern Colorado Springs as much as three feet of snow fell.  In the Black Forest and Monument three to four foot amounts were the norm.  At Palmer Lake, on the Palmer Divide, 52 inches of snow was reported.  The deep snow was blown and piled into drifts anywhere between three and 15 feet deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system combined with upslope northerly winds at the surface to produce widespread snow across the southeast mountains and adjoining plains starting the morning of the 24th and lasting into Christmas morning.  Storm totals ranged from 4 to 9 inches in and around the Wet Mountains.  However, 12 inches of snow fell in Cuchara in the Southern Sangre De Cristo Mountains and in Walsenburg in Huerfano county. CZ_NAME: SOUTHERN FRONT RANGE FOOTHILLS/CLEAR CREEK BASIN / W JEFFERSON / NE PARK / E CLEAR CREEK / SE GILPIN / SW DOUGLAS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of high winds occurred over portions of north central and northeast Colorado.  Electrical outages were reported in Breckenridge as some power lines were downed.  High wind reports included:  84 mph (73 kts) at Carter Lake, 71 mph (62 kts) at Blue Mountain, Red Feather Lakes and 4 miles south of Livermore, 70 mph (61 kts) at Breckenridge and Broomfield, with 59 mph (51 kts) reported 4 miles east of Haxtun.

A weather disturbance moved northeast across northern New Mexico, bringing snow to the mountains, high valleys, and plains of south central and southeast Colorado starting the morning of the 2nd through the afternoon of the 3rd.  Snow accumulations ranged from 4 to 6 inches across parts of the southeast plains, the Palmer Divide, and southern portions of the San Luis Valley.  Snowfall reached 9 inches at Rye in the Wet Mountains, 17 inches at Cuchara in the Southern Sangre De Cristo Mountains, and 22 inches at Wolf Creek Ski Area in the Eastern San Juan Mountains.  The rest of the southeast plains received 1 to 3 inches amounts, including much of the Colorado Springs and Pueblo areas.

A surface low pressure system moved across New Mexico into the Texas panhandle, setting down a band of heavy snow in extreme southeast Colorado.  Snowfall accumulations ranged from 4 to 8 inches from the Lamar area in northern Prowers county, throughout Baca county, including Springfield and Walsh, and eastern Las Animas county, including Kim.  One to three inches amounts were common in Kiowa, Bent, Crowley, Otero, and western Las Animas counties.

CZ_NAME: PUEBLO AND EASTERN FREMONT COUNTIES/PUEBLO AND VICINITY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific weather system moved across the Rockies and into the central plains of the U.S.from the 8th through the 10th. Abundant moisture was wrapped around the system, and brought snow to the southwestern and south central mountains the afternoon of the 8th through the 10th...and to the southeastern plains the morning of the 9th through the 10th.  Totals from a persistent band of snow ranged from 4 inches at the Pueblo Memorial Airport to 7 inches at Manzanola and Swink in Otero county. Fort Reynolds in Pueblo county reported 9 1/2 inches of accumulation by the afternoon of the 9th.  Across Monument Ridge, north of Colorado Springs, storm totals ranged from 10 to 18 inches in the Monument and Black Forest region east into northeast El Paso county.  Near blizzard conditions were experienced during the afternoon of the 10th in eastern El Paso county, forcing U.S. Highway 24 to close for a time.  Other snowfall totals included around one foot at Beulah and 20 to 25 inches around Rye in Pueblo county in the Wet Mountains.  Twenty two inches fell at Aspen Acres Campground in Custer county, and a remarkable 44 inches at Cuchara at 9,000 feet in the Southern Sangre De Cristo Mountains.

A strong winter storm system moved into Colorado from the west, bringing an abundance of moisture and gusty winds from 20 to 40 mph. The storm brought accumulations of 12 to 20 inches to the Eastern San Juan and La Garita mountains during the morning and afternoon of the 27th. The San Luis valley received 6 to 18 inches of snow the afternoon and evening of the 27th. The system then moved east and brought heavy snow to the Sangre de Cristo mountains and adjacent foothills and plains the evening of the 27th into the night of the 28th. A band of intense snowfall lasted for many hours over the Wet mountains, Wet Mountain Valley, southern Sangre de Cristo mountains, and Walsenburg area.  Total accumulations in those areas ranged from around 30 inches in the Wet Mountain Valley, around three feet in the southern Sangre de Cristo mountains and La Veta Pass, and at Walsenburg, Rye, and Beulah, to 45 inches at the 9,000 foot level in the Wet Mountains.  Nearly 30 inches of snow fell near Divide in Teller county.  Eight to 10 inches fell along the Palmer Divide, with generally less than 5 inches in the Colorado Springs area.  Pueblo tallied about 5 inches. The severe winter conditions closed numerous roads and highways, including Interstate 25 from Pueblo south to the New Mexico state line. Four thousand homes were without power in Pueblo West and Turkey Creek in Pueblo county when strong winds and heavy wet snow brought down hundreds of power poles. Ninety-two year old Marie Brandenburg of Westcliffe in Custer county died when she accidently locked herself out of her house, then slipped and fell by her back porch.  She froze to death in the high winds and heavy snow.

A powerful storm system brought sustained winds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts above 60 mph to the Rampart Range, Wet Mountains, and Spanish Peaks, and to the adjacent plains.  Measured winds gusted to around 60 mph in parts of Colorado Springs.  In Florence, in Fremont county, around 11:30 a.m., a tree fell on a 100-year old house causing heavy damage.  A wind gust of 103 mph occurred around 4:00 p.m. in Pueblo West, breaking windows.  A display sign was destroyed at the Pueblo Mall, and damaged several vehicles underneath it.  A few trees were uprooted and scattered power outages were reported around the area.

Snowfall of 1 to 2 feet combined with north winds 50 to 60 mph created widespread blizzard conditions with near zero visibility and wind chills to 20 degrees below zero.  Specific snowfall amounts were Wray 7 inches, Yuma 7, Joes 14, Idalia 12, Bonny Dam 13, Stratton 29, Burlington 22, Kit Carson 15, Kit Carson 9NNE 24, Cheyenne Wells 11, Arapahoe 16.An 11 year old boy perished in the storm after wandering away from his home near Stratton and becoming disoriented on the 25th.  His body was found on the 26th 1.5 miles southeast of his home.  Cause of death was hypothermia. An estimated 1100 head of cattle were killed.  Roads were closed and motorists stranded.  All activities were postponed or cancelled.  Many schools were closed for 1 to 2 days afterwards.

Fort Reynolds in Pueblo county reported 9 1/2 inches of accumulation by the afternoon of the 9th.  Across Monument Ridge, north of Colorado Springs, storm totals ranged from 10 to 18 inches in the Monument and Black Forest region east into northeast El Paso county.  Near blizzard conditions were experienced during the afternoon of the 10th in eastern El Paso county, forcing U.S. Highway 24 to close for a time.  Other snowfall totals included around one foot at Beulah and 20 to 25 inches around Rye in Pueblo county in the Wet Mountains.  Twenty two inches fell at Aspen Acres Campground in Custer county, and a remarkable 44 inches at Cuchara at 9,000 feet in the Southern Sangre De Cristo Mountains.

CZ_NAME: ELKHEAD AND PARK MOUNTAINS/FLAT TOPS/WHITE RIVER PLATEAU / E RIO BLANCO / NE GARFIELD / X NW EAGLE / ROUTT EXCEPT C XS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and moist Pacific trough produced 6 to 12 inches of snow in most mountain areas of western Colorado.  Heavier totals included 17 inches at Purgatory Ski Area, 14 inches at Rio Grande Reservoir,  14 inches at Crested Butte, 13 inches at Red Mountain Pass, and 13 inches at Snowmass Ski Area, Additionally, strong winds produced areas of blowing and drifting snow.  Some peak wind gusts at mountain top locations included 82 mph at Telluride and 66 MPH at Snowmass.

An upper level low pressure system combined with upslope northerly winds at the surface to produce widespread snow across the southeast mountains and adjoining plains starting the morning of the 24th and lasting into Christmas morning.  Storm totals ranged from 4 to 9 inches in and around the Wet Mountains.  However, 12 inches of snow fell in Cuchara in the Southern Sangre De Cristo Mountains and in Walsenburg in Huerfano county.  One to three inches of snow fell on Monument Ridge, north of Colorado Springs, and in parts of the adjacent plains in southeast Colorado.  One fatality was attributed to this storm.  James Hall, 83, of Wetmore in Custer county, was found Christmas Eve after apparently falling  outside of his home and dying of exposure.
Lightning struck and damaged a tree on the Grand Junction High School campus.
Lightning struck a house on the north side of the Grand Mesa, destroying some electrical items and blackening a wall on the side of the house.

CZ_NAME: UPPER HUERFANO RIVER BASIN/WALSENBURG AND VICINITY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system combined with arctic air to bring widespread snowfall to south central and southeast Colorado, starting the evening of the 13th and lasting through the afternoon of the 14th. Snowfall accumulations ranged from 6 to 12 inches across the mountains and high valleys, with 4 to 8 inches across the foothills and nearby plains.  The heaviest amounts occurred in the southern Sangre de Cristo mountains where up to 12 inches fell around Cuchara and Stonewall.  A narrow band of intense snow fell near Del Norte in the San Luis Valley, bringing up to 10 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Another supercell thunderstorm brought large hail and a tornado to southeast Otero county.  The storm and accompanying tornado moved out of open country southwest of the Higbee Valley and struck several ranches just north of the Purgatoire River on Road 804.  At about 8:45 p.m. the tornado struck three ranches about 3 miles southwest of Higbee.  Some barns were destroyed, and roofs were ripped off others.  Horse trailers, windmills, feed troughs, hay feeders, water tanks, grain storage bins were destroyed.  Many large cottonwood trees were broken off or uprooted.  The tornado ripped through the northwest side of Higbee, and one horse perished from injuries due to flying debris there.  The tornado then moved northeast through open ranch country for another 5 miles, with the only known damage being two downed power poles.

A brief tornado was reported by a Crowley county sheriffs officer on the outskirts of Crowley.  There was no known damage.  The brief, weak tornado was associated with the line of thunderstorms to the west of several supercell storms.

CZ_NAME: NORTHERN FRONT RANGE FOOTHILLS/UPPER LARAMIE AND CACHE LA POUDRE RIVER BASINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Damaging wind ahead of an approaching storm system developed in the Front Range Foothills in the  morning, spreading out across portions of the Urban Corridor and Northeast Plains during the afternoon and early evening.  Several trees and street signs were blown down with scattered power outages reported throughout the Denver Metropolitan area.  In Arvada,  northwest of Denver, a car window was blown out by a strong wind gust. Peak wind gust reports included:  88 mph (76 kts) 8 miles west of Conifer, 71 mph (62 kts) at the NCAR facility, in the foothills southwest of Boulder, 70 mph (61 kts) at Kiowa, 59 mph (51 kts) at Limon and 58 mph (50 kts) 9 miles north of Limon and 4 miles east of Haxtun. In addition to the strong winds ahead of the storm system,  the atmosphere was very unstable.  Three severe  thunderstorms produced large hail across portions of northeast Colorado.  No damage was reported.

CZ_NAME: SOUTHERN FRONT RANGE FOOTHILLS/CLEAR CREEK BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep east to northeast flow associated with a vigorous upper level low pressure system over the Four Corners area, combined with a strong Arctic airmass in the central plains, to create deadly blizzard conditions across all of eastern Colorado.  Snowfall totals along the Urban Corridor and adjacent plains generally ranged from 14 to 30 inches.  The heaviest snowfall occurred in the foothills west and southwest of Denver where 2 to 4 feet of snow was reported.  In addition, sustained winds to 40 mph (35 kts) with gusts as high as 60 mph (52 kts) caused zero visibilities and deadly wind chill temperatures to dip between 25 below and 40 below zero.  The blizzard stirred up snowdrifts ranging from 4 to 10 feet in depth.  Several major highways and interstates were closed as conditions became life threatening and travel impossible.  Red Cross shelters were set up for hundreds of travellers who became stranded and were forced to abandon their vehicles.  In the mountains, sustained winds to 70 mph (61 kts) with peak wind gusts to 110 mph (96 kts) were recorded above 12,000 feet at the Breckenridge Ski Area.  The blizzard was responsible, either directly or indirectly, for the deaths of 4 people in northeast Colorado.  One man died of carbon monoxide poisoning when he became stranded in his vehicle and the snow clogged the exhaust pipe.  A woman died when she lost control of the car in the blinding storm.  In addition, 2 elderly men died from heart attacks while shovelling snow.At Denver International Airport (DIA), 4,000 travellers were stranded when the airport was forced to shut down.  At least 120 cars were abandoned along Pena Blvd, the only arterial leading into and out of DIA.  The blizzard cost air carriers at least $20 million.  In Weld County, nearly 3,000 head of cattle died in the storm resulting in an estimated $1.5 million loss.Some of the more impressive snowfall totals included:  51 inches at Coal Creek Canyon, 48 inches at Silver Spruce Ranch and Monument Hill, 42 inches at Intercanyon, 37 inches at Sedalia, 36 inches at Foxton, 35 inches at Aspen Springs and Conifer, 32 inches at Elizabeth, 31 inches at Eldorado Springs, southeast Aurora and Englewood, with 30 inches reported at Allenspark, Sunshine Canyon and Table Mesa near Boulder.

A rogue gust of wind which travelled from northwest to southeast through the Leadville area blew down over a hundred trees, caving in the roof of a car and crashing through the roofs of three mobile homes.  Metal roofs and shingles were blown off of many buildings.  The fallen conifer trees were generally 60 feet tall and were snapped off 15 to 20 feet above the ground, except for a few which were uprooted.

Strong winds associated with the passage of a cold front blew down several large trees and broke off many large branches in portions of the Grand Junction metropolitan area.  A  house in Clifton was damaged when a tree toppled onto it.

The same supercell thunderstorm that brought large hail and a brief tornado to eastern Otero county produced a long track tornado through northwestern Bent and central Kiowa counties.  The tornado began about 7 miles west of Las Animas.  Eleven people were in the direct path of the tornado as it hit a ranch house and adjacent outbuildings.  Five men were caught outside, trying to move their vehicles from harm's way.   Four were thrown about by the tornado and received minor injuries.  Six women in the ranch house were unhurt.  The tornado moved northeast and blew a vehicle off Highway 194.  The occupant received minor injuries.  It destroyed a stream level monitor on the other side of the highway, then ripped through a line of trees before moving into barren country.  Eyewitnesses said the tornado remained intact for most of the 35 mile track northeast to just south of Highway 96 in Kiowa county.  Owing to the nearly uninhabited, barren landscape, little if any damage occurred beyond Highway 194 in Bent county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moving east through western Bent county merged with a northeast moving supercell thunderstorm just west of Las Animas. A tornado and high winds skipped across the landscape for about 8 miles from about 3 miles west northwest of Las Animas through ranch country of north central Bent county.  Windows were broken and roofs were destroyed on houses, outbuilding were damaged and destroyed, and vehicles were damaged and destroyed by both the high winds and hail.

A severe thunderstorm quickly developed along a line of thunderstorms moving east through southeast Colorado.  The high winds seems to be shortlived and a couple of trampolines were damaged and destroyed.  No damage to structures was reported.

Heavy rains resulted in several mud slides along a three mile stretch of railroad track in the Animas Valley.  Boulders up to 3 feet in diameter had to be removed from the tracks.
Heavy rains resulted in a flash flood which washed out a section of county road 201, stranding people at a nearby campground.
Heavy rains resulted in a mud slide which blocked County Road 213.

Strong prefrontal winds blew down numerous Spruce and Douglas Fir trees within an old growth forest of the Weminuche Wilderness of southeast San Juan County.  The diameter of the fallen trees ranged from 2 feet to 3.5 feet in diameter.  Most of the downed trees were snapped off several feet above the ground while a smaller number were uprooted.  Numerous trails in the wilderness area were blocked by the fallen trees.  Several high quality dome tents were destroyed when their flexible support poles were snapped and the tent fabric was shredded.

Heavier amounts included 10 inches at Climax, 11 inches at Mesa Lakes on the Grand Mesa, and 15 inches at Chattanooga on the south side of Red Mountain Pass.

CZ_NAME: CROWLEY AND OTERO COUNTIES/LA JUNTA AND VICINITY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While blizzards are not uncommon in Colorado, the widespread areal extent of this blizzard was, perhaps a one in 50 year event.  A powerful low pressure system moved in from the northwest through Utah, and settled into northern New Mexico.  Meantime, arctic air invaded eastern Colorado, setting the stage for high winds, below zero wind chill values, heavy snow, and widespread life threatening conditions.  The heavy snow began in the southwest mountains early on Friday morning the 24th.  Snow spread across the Eastern San Juan and La Garita Mountains into the San Luis Valley, Upper Arkansas Valley, Wet Mountain Valley, and Sangre de Cristo Mountains through Friday morning.  Snow began spreading across the eastern plains by midday Friday.  By Friday evening the arctic high pressure and low pressure to the south intensified, and blizzard conditions began in the eastern mountains and across the plains.  Blizzard conditions persisted, except for a brief break early Saturday morning, through Saturday night into the extreme eastern plains.  The scope and length of blizzard conditions proved fatal for several eastern Coloradans.  Three people in El Paso county perished from carbon monoxide poisoning after waiting for help to come in their snowbound vehicles for over 24 hours.  Another person froze to death in a vehicle on post at Fort Carson in the Colorado Springs area.  An elderly women in Otero county tried to walk home after her vehicle became stuck in open country.  She froze to death.  A man in Bent county froze to death in open country while hunting, or looking for other hunters.   Another man died in a vehicle accident in Pueblo during the blizzard Friday night.  Two people were injured in Colorado Springs when a canopy at a gas station collapsed under the weight of deep snow on top.  Another canopy at a gas station in Lamar collapsed, but noone was injured.  A vehicle was destroyed, though.  Thousands of people were stranded in eastern Colorado, and hundreds had to be rescued from their snowbound vehicles.  By Saturday, the Governor declared a State of Emergency.  Emergency traffic only was allowed on eastern Colorado roadways.  Rescues were made by the U.S. Army in Humvees and by helicopter, the National Guard, law enforcement, other public resources, and private citizens.  The combination of high wind and heavy snow caused power lines to come down.  Power outages occurred (and lasted up to two days) in many parts of the area, most notably in parts of Colorado Springs, in and around Pueblo, and in southeast Colorado from McClave in Bent county to Holly in Prowers county.  Hundreds of businesses and stores were closed throughout much of the weekend, and temporary sales and production loses were in the millions of dollars.  Many school districts were closed for a part, or all of the following week, as the digging out process continued.  Extra costs for snow removel in both the private and public sector from overtime wages certainly ran into the millions of dollars areawide.  Not only did humans suffer, but there was a widespread die off of range cattle.  Estimates of around 20,000 dead cattle and calves were given.  Untold numbers were saved by hay drops carried out by the Army and National Guard.  Removal of dead cattle and feeding of remaining cattle cost millions of dollars.  Snow amounts with the blizzard were impressive.  One to two feet fell in the Eastern San Juan, La Garita, and Sangre de Cristo Mountains.  However, the San Luis Valley received only a few inches of snow.  The Upper Arkansas Valley had six to 12 inches of snow.  Generally between one and two feet fell from the Rampart Range and southern Colorado Springs, south through Pueblo, down to Trinidad, and throughout the southeast Plains.  However, parts of the Wet Mountains, Beulah, Walsenburg, and some areas around La Junta had around three feet of snow.  From northern Colorado Springs to the Palmer Divide, amounts were excessive.  In northern Colorado Springs as much as three feet of snow fell.  In the Black Forest and Monument three to four foot amounts were the norm.  At Palmer Lake, on the Palmer Divide, 52 inches of snow was reported.  The deep snow was blown and piled into drifts anywhere between three and 15 feet deep.

Three people in El Paso county perished from carbon monoxide poisoning after waiting for help to come in their snowbound vehicles for over 24 hours.  Another person froze to death in a vehicle on post at Fort Carson in the Colorado Springs area.  An elderly women in Otero county tried to walk home after her vehicle became stuck in open country.  She froze to death.  A man in Bent county froze to death in open country while hunting, or looking for other hunters.   Another man died in a vehicle accident in Pueblo during the blizzard Friday night.  Two people were injured in Colorado Springs when a canopy at a gas station collapsed under the weight of deep snow on top.  Another canopy at a gas station in Lamar collapsed, but noone was injured.  A vehicle was destroyed, though.  Thousands of people were stranded in eastern Colorado, and hundreds had to be rescued from their snowbound vehicles.  By Saturday, the Governor declared a State of Emergency.  Emergency traffic only was allowed on eastern Colorado roadways.  Rescues were made by the U.S. Army in Humvees and by helicopter, the National Guard, law enforcement, other public resources, and private citizens.  The combination of high wind and heavy snow caused power lines to come down.  Power outages occurred (and lasted up to two days) in many parts of the area, most notably in parts of Colorado Springs, in and around Pueblo, and in southeast Colorado from McClave in Bent county to Holly in Prowers county.  Hundreds of businesses and stores were closed throughout much of the weekend, and temporary sales and production loses were in the millions of dollars.  Many school districts were closed for a part, or all of the following week, as the digging out process continued.  Extra costs for snow removel in both the private and public sector from overtime wages certainly ran into the millions of dollars areawide.  Not only did humans suffer, but there was a widespread die off of range cattle.  Estimates of around 20,000 dead cattle and calves were given.  Untold numbers were saved by hay drops carried out by the Army and National Guard.  Removal of dead cattle and feeding of remaining cattle cost millions of dollars.  Snow amounts with the blizzard were impressive.  One to two feet fell in the Eastern San Juan, La Garita, and Sangre de Cristo Mountains.  However, the San Luis Valley received only a few inches of snow.  The Upper Arkansas Valley had six to 12 inches of snow.  Generally between one and two feet fell from the Rampart Range and southern Colorado Springs, south through Pueblo, down to Trinidad, and throughout the southeast Plains.  However, parts of the Wet Mountains, Beulah, Walsenburg, and some areas around La Junta had around three feet of snow.  From northern Colorado Springs to the Palmer Divide, amounts were excessive.  In northern Colorado Springs as much as three feet of snow fell.  In the Black Forest and Monument three to four foot amounts were the norm.  At Palmer Lake, on the Palmer Divide, 52 inches of snow was reported.  The deep snow was blown and piled into drifts anywhere between three and 15 feet deep.

Very strong jet stream winds worked down to mountain top, then into the plains.  Sustained winds blew from 35 to 45 mph, with gusts to 70 mph in the mountains and 60 mph in the adjacent plains.  No known major damage was reported.

Lightning struck two power poles at Fruita Middle School, resulting in minor electrical damage.  Several cheerleaders at a nearby football game were knocked to the ground by the shock wave.

A few mountain reporting stations had storm totals ranging from 12 to 14 inches.  Wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph were reported at some mountain locations, resulting in blowing and drifting snow.

Strong outflow winds from a supercell thunderstorm were estimated to have gusts at least to 80 MPH.  Most 4 by 4 foot wooden sign posts were snapped off within Rangely as well as along highway 64 and highway 139 in the Rangely area.  Many steel sign posts were bent over  and a few were snapped off.  A 2 foot diameter cottonwood tree was blown over and crushed a late model van.  Some other large trees were blown over, along with many large tree branches.  A metal storage shed was ripped from its moorings and blown over the side of a hill.

A strong winter storm system moved into Colorado from the west, bringing an abundance of moisture and gusty winds from 20 to 40 mph. The storm brought accumulations of 12 to 20 inches to the Eastern San Juan and La Garita mountains during the morning and afternoon of the 27th. The San Luis valley received 6 to 18 inches of snow the afternoon and evening of the 27th. The system then moved east and brought heavy snow to the Sangre de Cristo mountains and adjacent foothills and plains the evening of the 27th into the night of the 28th. A band of intense snowfall lasted for many hours over the Wet mountains, Wet Mountain Valley, southern Sangre de Cristo mountains, and Walsenburg area.  Total accumulations in those areas ranged from around 30 inches in the Wet Mountain Valley, around three feet in the southern Sangre de Cristo mountains and La Veta Pass, and at Walsenburg, Rye, and Beulah, to 45 inches at the 9,000 foot level in the Wet Mountains.  Nearly 30 inches of snow fell near Divide in Teller county.  Eight to 10 inches fell along the Palmer Divide, with generally less than 5 inches in the Colorado Springs area.  Pueblo tallied about 5 inches. The severe winter conditions closed numerous roads and highways, including Interstate 25 from Pueblo south to the New Mexico state line. Four thousand homes were without power in Pueblo West and Turkey Creek in Pueblo county when strong winds and heavy wet snow brought down hundreds of power poles. Ninety-two year old Marie Brandenburg of Westcliffe in Custer county died when she accidently locked herself out of her house, then slipped and fell by her back porch.  She froze to death in the high winds and heavy snow.

The same supercell thunderstorm that brought large hail to the La Junta area, generated a brief tornado in a rural area of northeast Otero county.  It broke off a power pole and caused no other known damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 1 to 2 feet combined with north winds 50 to 60 mph created widespread blizzard conditions with near zero visibility and wind chills to 20 degrees below zero.  Specific snowfall amounts were Wray 7 inches, Yuma 7, Joes 14, Idalia 12, Bonny Dam 13, Stratton 29, Burlington 22, Kit Carson 15, Kit Carson 9NNE 24, Cheyenne Wells 11, Arapahoe 16.An 11 year old boy perished in the storm after wandering away from his home near Stratton and becoming disoriented on the 25th.  His body was found on the 26th 1.5 miles southeast of his home.  Cause of death was hypothermia. An estimated 1100 head of cattle were killed.  Roads were closed and motorists stranded.  All activities were postponed or cancelled.  Many schools were closed for 1 to 2 days afterwards.

CZ_NAME: UPPER HUERFANO RIVER BASIN/WALSENBURG AND VICINITY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system combined with upslope northerly winds at the surface to produce widespread snow across the southeast mountains and adjoining plains starting the morning of the 24th and lasting into Christmas morning.  Storm totals ranged from 4 to 9 inches in and around the Wet Mountains.  However, 12 inches of snow fell in Cuchara in the Southern Sangre De Cristo Mountains and in Walsenburg in Huerfano county.  One to three inches of snow fell on Monument Ridge, north of Colorado Springs, and in parts of the adjacent plains in southeast Colorado.  One fatality was attributed to this storm.  James Hall, 83, of Wetmore in Custer county, was found Christmas Eve after apparently falling  outside of his home and dying of exposure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving Pacific trough produced 4 to 7 inches of snow in most mountain areas of western Colorado as well as a few valleys above the 6000 foot level.  The heaviest reported accumulation was in the northern Uncompahgre Plateau with about a foot of new snow.

Over an inch of rain fell in Gateway within an hour.  Resultant flash floods damaged some irrigation projects and caused mud slides across highway 141.  A diesel tractor-trailer rig jackknifed in a mud flow on the highway about a mile south of Gateway.  West Creek rose over 10 feet, inundating a private bridge on the southeast side of Gateway.
Lightning struck and killed a Palomino quarter horse.  The lightning also ignited a fire on a nearby ten ton haystack.

A powerful storm system brought sustained winds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts above 60 mph to the Rampart Range, Wet Mountains, and Spanish Peaks, and to the adjacent plains.  Measured winds gusted to around 60 mph in parts of Colorado Springs.  In Florence, in Fremont county, around 11:30 a.m., a tree fell on a 100-year old house causing heavy damage.  A wind gust of 103 mph occurred around 4:00 p.m. in Pueblo West, breaking windows.  A display sign was destroyed at the Pueblo Mall, and damaged several vehicles underneath it.  A few trees were uprooted and scattered power outages were reported around the area.

CZ_NAME: ELKHEAD AND PARK MOUNTAINS/FLAT TOPS/WHITE RIVER PLATEAU / E RIO BLANCO / NE GARFIELD / X NW EAGLE / ROUTT EXCEPT C XS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A blast of wind from the east screamed down the west side of the Continental Divide and leveled over 20,000 acres of old-growth forest in the Mount Zirkel Wilderness and also in the Routt National Forest.  The discontinuous area with trees blown down stretched from 5 miles north of Steamboat Springs to about 26 miles north of Steamboat Springs.  The trees were typically blown down in tracts ranging from 50 acres to 300 acres.  Most of the trees were 2-3 feet diameter Spruce and were snapped off several feet above the ground.  Some stands of large Aspen trees were also blown down well above the trunk.  All of the fallen trees were laying in the same direction.  Many hunters were trapped for up to two days due to trees blocking the roads.  Miraculously, only minor damage was reported to vehicles and cabins.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The same supercell thunderstorm which spawned a tornado in southeast Otero county in the Higbee Valley dropped a brief, weak tornado west southwest of Las Animas in a ranch land area.  Grass was laid down, but there was no known property or crop damage.  Knowledge of this and other supercell tornadic thunderstorms moving east of La Junta prompted law enforcement to stop an eastbound Amtrak train and evacuate the 300 passengers around 9:00 p.m.  The train was allowed to travel on to Chicago around 11:00 p.m.

The supercell thunderstorm that struck central Las Animas county with high winds and a probable tornado weakened as it moved northeast through Otero county.  But then it became severe and produced 3/4 inch diameter hail just to the southeast of La Junta, and a 58 mph wind gust at Cheraw in northeast Otero county.  There was no known damage in Cheraw.
A thunderstorm , embedded in a long line of storms, became severe and dropped some large hail over a small area.  There was no known property or crop damage.

CZ_NAME: WESTERN FREMONT AND NORTHERN CUSTER COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific weather system moved across the Rockies and into the central plains of the U.S.from the 8th through the 10th. Abundant moisture was wrapped around the system, and brought snow to the southwestern and south central mountains the afternoon of the 8th through the 10th...and to the southeastern plains the morning of the 9th through the 10th.  Totals from a persistent band of snow ranged from 4 inches at the Pueblo Memorial Airport to 7 inches at Manzanola and Swink in Otero county. Fort Reynolds in Pueblo county reported 9 1/2 inches of accumulation by the afternoon of the 9th.  Across Monument Ridge, north of Colorado Springs, storm totals ranged from 10 to 18 inches in the Monument and Black Forest region east into northeast El Paso county.  Near blizzard conditions were experienced during the afternoon of the 10th in eastern El Paso county, forcing U.S. Highway 24 to close for a time.  Other snowfall totals included around one foot at Beulah and 20 to 25 inches around Rye in Pueblo county in the Wet Mountains.  Twenty two inches fell at Aspen Acres Campground in Custer county, and a remarkable 44 inches at Cuchara at 9,000 feet in the Southern Sangre De Cristo Mountains.

A moist northwest flow resulted in 4 to 8 inches of new snow over many mountain areas of western Colorado, and 3 to 5 inches in some northwest valleys.  Heavier snowfall reports included 14 inches at Columbine, 13 inches at Climax, and 10 inches at Vail Ski Area.

An upper level low pressure system combined with upslope northerly winds at the surface to produce widespread snow across the southeast mountains and adjoining plains starting the morning of the 24th and lasting into Christmas morning.  Storm totals ranged from 4 to 9 inches in and around the Wet Mountains.  However, 12 inches of snow fell in Cuchara in the Southern Sangre De Cristo Mountains and in Walsenburg in Huerfano county.  One to three inches of snow fell on Monument Ridge, north of Colorado Springs, and in parts of the adjacent plains in southeast Colorado.  One fatality was attributed to this storm.  James Hall, 83, of Wetmore in Custer county, was found Christmas Eve after apparently falling  outside of his home and dying of exposure.

Another supercell thunderstorm, from radar indications, probably spawned a tornado in the Purgatoire River Valley north of U.S. Highway 160 15 to 20 miles east northeast of Trinidad.  The area the storm passed through is uninhabited, with no roads, and little vegetation to point out a tornado track.  However, gusty winds to 66 mph were measured on the rear flank of the storm south of the probable tornado.

The same supercell thunderstorm that brought large hail and a brief tornado to eastern Otero county produced a long track tornado through northwestern Bent and central Kiowa counties.  The tornado began about 7 miles west of Las Animas.  Eleven people were in the direct path of the tornado as it hit a ranch house and adjacent outbuildings.  Five men were caught outside, trying to move their vehicles from harm's way.   Four were thrown about by the tornado and received minor injuries.  Six women in the ranch house were unhurt.  The tornado moved northeast and blew a vehicle off Highway 194.  The occupant received minor injuries.  It destroyed a stream level monitor on the other side of the highway, then ripped through a line of trees before moving into barren country.  Eyewitnesses said the tornado remained intact for most of the 35 mile track northeast to just south of Highway 96 in Kiowa county.  Owing to the nearly uninhabited, barren landscape, little if any damage occurred beyond Highway 194 in Bent county.

About an inch of rain fell within 20 minutes resulting in areas of street flooding.  A canal was unable to contain the high runoff and resulted in flooding of a business area with muddy water 2 to 3 feet deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and moist Pacific trough produced 6 to 12 inches of snow in most mountain areas of western Colorado.  Heavier totals included 17 inches at Purgatory Ski Area, 14 inches at Rio Grande Reservoir,  14 inches at Crested Butte, 13 inches at Red Mountain Pass, and 13 inches at Snowmass Ski Area, Additionally, strong winds produced areas of blowing and drifting snow.  Some peak wind gusts at mountain top locations included 82 mph at Telluride and 66 MPH at Snowmass.

Heavy rains on the Bookcliff Mountains raised the water level by 6 feet in Government Creek and sent 1 to 2 feet of water roaring into the western side of Rifle.  Some businesses and about a dozen homes received flood damage.  In the aftermath, rocks, mud, and other debris had to be removed from roads and lawns.
Heavy rains brought a flash flood which washed out a section of County Road 102.

Strong prefrontal winds blew down numerous large Aspen and Pine trees on the Grand Mesa.  Many trees were snapped off 3 to 4 feet above the ground.  One tree fell over the hood of a pickup.  Power lines were felled and roads were blocked by the fallen trees.

CZ_NAME: UPPER YAMPA RIVER BASIN/STEAMBOAT SPRINGS AND VICINITY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific trough resulted in 4 to 10 inches of snow in the western Colorado mountains above the 7000 foot level and 3 to 8 inches of snow in many valleys between 5000 and 7000 feet above sea level.  Some heavier totals included 24 inches at Buffalo Pass northeast of Steamboat Springs, 17 inches above Aspen, 14 inches at Chattanooga on the south side of Red Mountain Pass, 13 inches on the Grand Mesa, 13 inches at Climax, and 12 inches at Crested Butte.

CZ_NAME: EASTERN DOUGLAS AND WESTERN ELBERT COUNTIES/CASTLE ROCK AND VICINITY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Damaging wind ahead of an approaching storm system developed in the Front Range Foothills in the  morning, spreading out across portions of the Urban Corridor and Northeast Plains during the afternoon and early evening.  Several trees and street signs were blown down with scattered power outages reported throughout the Denver Metropolitan area.  In Arvada,  northwest of Denver, a car window was blown out by a strong wind gust. Peak wind gust reports included:  88 mph (76 kts) 8 miles west of Conifer, 71 mph (62 kts) at the NCAR facility, in the foothills southwest of Boulder, 70 mph (61 kts) at Kiowa, 59 mph (51 kts) at Limon and 58 mph (50 kts) 9 miles north of Limon and 4 miles east of Haxtun. In addition to the strong winds ahead of the storm system,  the atmosphere was very unstable.  Three severe  thunderstorms produced large hail across portions of northeast Colorado.  No damage was reported.

CZ_NAME: UPPER HUERFANO RIVER BASIN/WALSENBURG AND VICINITY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm system moved into Colorado from the west, bringing an abundance of moisture and gusty winds from 20 to 40 mph. The storm brought accumulations of 12 to 20 inches to the Eastern San Juan and La Garita mountains during the morning and afternoon of the 27th. The San Luis valley received 6 to 18 inches of snow the afternoon and evening of the 27th. The system then moved east and brought heavy snow to the Sangre de Cristo mountains and adjacent foothills and plains the evening of the 27th into the night of the 28th. A band of intense snowfall lasted for many hours over the Wet mountains, Wet Mountain Valley, southern Sangre de Cristo mountains, and Walsenburg area.  Total accumulations in those areas ranged from around 30 inches in the Wet Mountain Valley, around three feet in the southern Sangre de Cristo mountains and La Veta Pass, and at Walsenburg, Rye, and Beulah, to 45 inches at the 9,000 foot level in the Wet Mountains.  Nearly 30 inches of snow fell near Divide in Teller county.  Eight to 10 inches fell along the Palmer Divide, with generally less than 5 inches in the Colorado Springs area.  Pueblo tallied about 5 inches. The severe winter conditions closed numerous roads and highways, including Interstate 25 from Pueblo south to the New Mexico state line. Four thousand homes were without power in Pueblo West and Turkey Creek in Pueblo county when strong winds and heavy wet snow brought down hundreds of power poles. Ninety-two year old Marie Brandenburg of Westcliffe in Custer county died when she accidently locked herself out of her house, then slipped and fell by her back porch.  She froze to death in the high winds and heavy snow.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS/ASPEN AND VICINITY / NE S EAGLE / PITKIN EXCEPT XNC / X NE MESA / SE GARFIELD (PART)
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist northwest flow resulted in 4 to 8 inches of new snow over many mountain areas of western Colorado, and 3 to 5 inches in some northwest valleys.  Heavier snowfall reports included 14 inches at Columbine, 13 inches at Climax, and 10 inches at Vail Ski Area.

CZ_NAME: WEST ELK AND SAWATCH MOUNTAINS/TAYLOR PARK / W LAKE / W CHAFFEE / WC TO EC GUNNISON
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist northwest flow resulted in 4 to 8 inches of new snow over many mountain areas of western Colorado, and 3 to 5 inches in some northwest valleys.  Heavier snowfall reports included 14 inches at Columbine, 13 inches at Climax, and 10 inches at Vail Ski Area.

CZ_NAME: UPPER PURGATOIRE RIVER BASIN/TRINIDAD AND VICINITY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weather disturbance moved northeast across northern New Mexico, bringing snow to the mountains, high valleys, and plains of south central and southeast Colorado starting the morning of the 2nd through the afternoon of the 3rd.  Snow accumulations ranged from 4 to 6 inches across parts of the southeast plains, the Palmer Divide, and southern portions of the San Luis Valley.  Snowfall reached 9 inches at Rye in the Wet Mountains, 17 inches at Cuchara in the Southern Sangre De Cristo Mountains, and 22 inches at Wolf Creek Ski Area in the Eastern San Juan Mountains.  The rest of the southeast plains received 1 to 3 inches amounts, including much of the Colorado Springs and Pueblo areas.

CZ_NAME: PUEBLO AND EASTERN FREMONT COUNTIES/PUEBLO AND VICINITY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system combined with upslope northerly winds at the surface to produce widespread snow across the southeast mountains and adjoining plains starting the morning of the 24th and lasting into Christmas morning.  Storm totals ranged from 4 to 9 inches in and around the Wet Mountains.  However, 12 inches of snow fell in Cuchara in the Southern Sangre De Cristo Mountains and in Walsenburg in Huerfano county.  One to three inches of snow fell on Monument Ridge, north of Colorado Springs, and in parts of the adjacent plains in southeast Colorado.  One fatality was attributed to this storm.  James Hall, 83, of Wetmore in Custer county, was found Christmas Eve after apparently falling  outside of his home and dying of exposure.

A moist Pacific weather system moved across the Rockies and into the central plains of the U.S.from the 8th through the 10th. Abundant moisture was wrapped around the system, and brought snow to the southwestern and south central mountains the afternoon of the 8th through the 10th...and to the southeastern plains the morning of the 9th through the 10th.  Totals from a persistent band of snow ranged from 4 inches at the Pueblo Memorial Airport to 7 inches at Manzanola and Swink in Otero county. Fort Reynolds in Pueblo county reported 9 1/2 inches of accumulation by the afternoon of the 9th.  Across Monument Ridge, north of Colorado Springs, storm totals ranged from 10 to 18 inches in the Monument and Black Forest region east into northeast El Paso county.  Near blizzard conditions were experienced during the afternoon of the 10th in eastern El Paso county, forcing U.S. Highway 24 to close for a time.  Other snowfall totals included around one foot at Beulah and 20 to 25 inches around Rye in Pueblo county in the Wet Mountains.  Twenty two inches fell at Aspen Acres Campground in Custer county, and a remarkable 44 inches at Cuchara at 9,000 feet in the Southern Sangre De Cristo Mountains.
